topic.what: Synthesis of the gut-brain axis, covering the enteric nervous system (ENS), vagus nerve signaling, and bidirectional microbiome-brain communication.
topic.how: Expert narrative synthesis drawing on neuroscience, gastroenterology, and microbiology research; explains how the ENS, vagus nerve, and gut microbiota collectively regulate mood, cognition, and gastric physiology.
topic.why_matters: Establishes a mechanistic framework linking psychological stress to gastric pathophysiology, providing the conceptual basis for understanding stress-induced gastritis and microbiome-mediated gut-brain disorders.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Breadth of systems integrated in the gut-brain axis model
characteristics.value: Integrates ENS, vagus nerve, HPA axis, and microbiome signaling into a unified framework
characteristics.vs_baseline: Prior models treated gut and brain as largely independent
characteristics.evidence: Expert synthesis by UCLA gastroenterologist Emeran Mayer with decades of clinical and research experience
characteristics.context: Book-length treatment; target audience includes clinicians and informed general readers

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree to which psychological stress alters gastric acid secretion, motility, and mucosal defense
characteristics.value: Stress activates HPA axis and autonomic nervous system, disrupting gastric mucosal integrity and motility
characteristics.evidence: Synthesis of animal and human studies on stress and gastric physiology
characteristics.context: Mechanism described qualitatively; quantitative magnitude depends on stressor type and duration

good_for: Building conceptual models of stress-induced gastritis
Understanding microbiome contributions to gastric inflammation
Informing integrative treatment approaches targeting gut-brain axis
not_for: Specific clinical dosing or treatment protocols
Primary empirical data on gastritis biomarkers
requires: Background in basic neuroscience or gastroenterology to evaluate mechanistic claims critically

novelty: Presents a unified gut-brain-microbiome framework at a time when microbiome-brain research was still emerging (2016)
Synthesizes ENS, vagus nerve, and microbiome signaling into a single coherent model accessible to non-specialists
open_problems: Causal directionality of microbiome-brain signaling remains incompletely resolved
Translating gut-brain axis insights into specific gastritis therapies requires further clinical trials
Individual variation in vagal tone and microbiome composition limits generalizability of the framework
